diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A seal-like barking cough is the hallmark of croup and strongly localises the pathology to the subglottic larynx.
A viral coryzal prodrome is the usual precursor of croup.
A clear viral prodrome argues against a sudden inhaled foreign body in a previously well child.

symptoms.symptom_name: Inspiratory stridor present only on agitation, absent at rest

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Stridor that appears only with agitation and settles at rest is characteristic of MILD croup (dynamic subglottic narrowing).
Laryngeal involvement produces hoarseness, consistent with croup and against a purely lower-airway process.

symptoms.symptom_name: Swallowing saliva, drinking, no drooling, no muffled voice, no tripod posture (pertinent negative)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: The ability to swallow saliva, absence of drooling and of a muffled voice or tripod posture are the key features that argue against epiglottitis.

symptoms.symptom_name: No stridor at rest and only crying-associated mild recession (pertinent negative for severe croup)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of stridor and increased work of breathing at rest grades this as MILD croup rather than moderate/severe.
A low-grade fever fits a viral illness; the absence of high toxic fever argues against epiglottitis and bacterial tracheitis.
Bacterial tracheitis typically causes high fever and systemic toxicity, absent here.

symptoms.symptom_name: Symptoms worse at night; minimal relief from steam (natural history)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Nocturnal worsening is typical of croup; humidified/steam air has no proven benefit, consistent with the reported minimal relief.

symptoms.symptom_name: Sub-therapeutic single paracetamol dose; no regular analgesia/antipyretic

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Analgesia/antipyretic history is a management detail, not a diagnostic discriminator; the definitive treatment for croup is corticosteroid, not paracetamol.

symptoms.symptom_name: No sudden choking event; no witnessed foreign-body ingestion (pertinent negative)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: The absence of a choking event and the presence of a viral prodrome argue strongly against an inhaled foreign body.

symptoms.symptom_name: Chest clear to auscultation, equal air entry, no wheeze or crepitations

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A clear chest localises the problem to the upper airway (subglottis) and argues against a lower respiratory tract infection or bronchospasm — but requires auscultation, impossible via telehealth chat.

symptoms.symptom_name: Clinician-measured vitals: HR 120, RR 28, SpO2 98%, temp 37.8 C

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Normal oxygen saturation and only mildly raised heart/respiratory rate support mild disease with adequate oxygenation — but these are clinician-measured and cannot be reliably obtained via telehealth chat without a device.

investigation_details.expected_result: Subglottic narrowing ('steeple sign') supportive of croup
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Not required and not recommended acutely — croup is a clinical diagnosis and imaging can distress the child and delay treatment; it adds nothing to the management of classic mild croup.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: The steeple sign is supportive but non-essential; croup is diagnosed clinically and imaging is not indicated in classic mild disease.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: A coryzal prodrome followed by a seal-like barking cough, hoarse voice and agitation-only inspiratory stridor together constitute a high-probability mild croup picture.

Preserved swallowing without drooling, no muffled voice or tripod posture, and no stridor/recession at rest exclude the dangerous mimics and grade the croup as mild, justifying same-day community management with airway safety-netting.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Same-day in-person clinical airway assessment (GP / after-hours GP / urgent care)
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: A clinician needs to confirm mild croup, assess the airway and work of breathing directly, and give a single dose of oral corticosteroid — none of which can be done over telehealth chat.
Not indicated for classic mild croup — it is a clinical diagnosis; imaging can distress the child, provoke agitation and delay treatment.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Throat examination / tongue-depressor inspection of the oropharynx
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Actively contraindicated when epiglottitis is in the differential and unnecessary in classic croup — it can provoke airway spasm; the airway is assessed by observation, not instrumentation.
